perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with or without cause by giving ten (10) days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with or without cause by giving one (1) day written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to one day may violate state laws requiring longer notice, especially if the agreement is construed as having aspects of an employment or services contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 6, Section 2-309 requires 'reasonable notification' when an agreement is terminated. One day's notice is unlikely to be considered 'reasonable' in many contexts, especially if significant reliance or investment has been made by either party. The original text may or may not be deemed reasonable, but the modified text is definitely not reasonable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for termination from ten days to one day. The Law Explanation states that Delaware Code Title 6, Section 2-309 requires 'reasonable notification' when an agreement is terminated, and that one day's notice is unlikely to be considered 'reasonable'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows Title 6, however the only relevant text talks about obligations and unconscionability. Since the Law Explanation states that the changed text is unreasonable, and the Perturbation Explanation says that the changed text may violate state laws, the reduction creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the Parties (which shall include only employees and directors of each Party but shall not include agents, representatives, consultants, or financial advisors, unless disclosure to any such non-employee or non-director has been approved prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party) are willing to exchange with each other pursuant to this Agreement business and technical information that is (a) disclosed in writing and marked with a suitable restrictive legend or, (b) if initially disclosed orally, is identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and within thirty (30) days after such oral disclosure is reduced to writing and marked with a suitable restrictive legend (collectively, such information being hereinafter referred to as “Confidential Information”);
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the Parties (which shall include only employees and directors of each Party but shall not include agents, representatives, consultants, or financial advisors, unless disclosure to any such non-employee or non-director has been approved prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party) are willing to exchange with each other pursuant to this Agreement business and technical information that is (a) disclosed in writing and marked with a suitable restrictive legend or, (b) if initially disclosed orally, is identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and within sixty (60) days after such oral disclosure is reduced to writing and marked with a suitable restrictive legend (collectively, such information being hereinafter referred to as “Confidential Information”);
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe for reducing orally disclosed confidential information to writing from 30 days to 60 days creates a contradiction if Delaware trade secret law requires that the information is properly identified as confidential within a specific, shorter timeframe to qualify for trade secret protection. By extending the deadline, the information might lose trade secret protection if not properly identified in writing sooner.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By extending the period allowed to reduce orally disclosed information to writing and identify it as confidential, there's an increased risk that the information is no longer treated with sufficient secrecy, which would undermine any claim for trade secret protection under DUTSA. Thirty days is an industry standard, modifying it to sixty days could be viewed as commercially unreasonable.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the timeframe for reducing orally disclosed confidential information to writing from 30 to 60 days. This creates an inconsistency because it potentially weakens the protection of trade secrets under Delaware law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Trade secret' as information that 'Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b.' Extending the timeframe increases the risk that the information loses its secrecy, contradicting the requirement to maintain its secrecy to qualify as a trade secret. While the snippet does not specify the exact timeframe, the extended period introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party under Section 2 shall survive termination of this Agreement and shall remain in effect until the earlier of: (a) the Disclosing Party, in its sole discretion, releases the Receiving Party by notification in writing that the Receiving Party is no longer required to protect the Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement; or (b) the Confidential Information falls within an exception set forth in Section 3; or (c) two (2) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party under Section 2 shall survive termination of this Agreement and shall remain in effect until the earlier of: (a) the Disclosing Party, in its sole discretion, releases the Receiving Party by notification in writing that the Receiving Party is no longer required to protect the Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement; or (b) the Confidential Information falls within an exception set forth in Section 3; or (c) twenty (20) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to twenty years could be deemed unreasonable and against public policy under Delaware law, particularly if it covers information that is not truly a trade secret or if it unduly restricts competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding unreasonable restraints on trade and duration of confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article I, Section 6 of the Delaware Constitution protects against unreasonable restraints on trade. A twenty-year confidentiality period, especially if applied broadly to non-trade secret information, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if the confidential information is not narrowly defined and the extended period significantly impairs the ability of the Receiving Party to engage in their profession or business. The original two-year restriction has a much better chance of being deemed reasonable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period from two years to twenty years. The Law Explanation states that under the Delaware Constitution Article I, Section 6, unreasonable restraints on trade are prohibited. The Law Explanation specifically mentions that a twenty-year confidentiality period, especially if applied broadly, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint. The original two-year restriction has a better chance of being deemed reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Article I of the Delaware Constitution which includes a bill of rights; this is enough to justify that such a law exists. Thus, increasing the confidentiality period to twenty years creates a legal contradiction by potentially creating an unreasonable restraint on trade, whereas the original contract likely did not.
